name: Paraguayan Chipa
steps: Mix the masa harina, cheese, egg, and melted butter into a soft, slightly sticky dough.
Shape into small rings or rolls.
Bake at 400F for 18-20 minutes until golden and set.
steps_detailed: Combine the masa harina with the crumbled queso fresco and grated cotija in a bowl.
Add the egg, melted butter, and milk, and mix until a soft, cohesive dough forms - it should hold together but still feel a little soft.
Shape into small rings, knots, or rolls about 2 inches across.
Bake at 400F (200C) for 18-22 minutes until golden brown and firm to the touch.
Best eaten warm, ideally with a cup of cocido (toasted mate) alongside.

name: Paraguayan Mbeju
steps: Mix the cassava starch with the crumbled cheese and enough milk to form a crumbly, just-moist mixture.
Press into a thin, even layer in a hot, lightly buttered skillet.
Cook a few minutes per side until golden and set, then fold in half like a flatbread.
steps_detailed: Combine the cassava starch and crumbled queso fresco in a bowl, rubbing them together with your fingers until evenly mixed.
Add the milk a little at a time, just enough to bring the mixture to a slightly damp, crumbly texture - it shouldn't form a solid dough.
Heat the butter in a nonstick skillet over medium heat, then press the mixture into a thin, even layer covering the pan.
Cook 3-4 minutes until the underside is golden and holds together, then carefully flip (or fold in half if it's fragile) and cook 2-3 more minutes.
Serve warm, folded like a quesadilla.

name: Paraguayan Cocido con Leche
steps: Warm the milk with the brown sugar and cinnamon until steaming but not boiling.
Traditionally brewed with toasted yerba mate and charred sugar for a smoky flavor - a strong black tea can stand in if you don't have yerba mate.
Serve hot in a mug.
steps_detailed: In a small pot, melt the brown sugar over medium heat until it just starts to darken and smell toasty - this mimics the charred sugar used in traditional cocido.
Carefully add the milk (it may bubble up) and the cinnamon, and warm through, stirring to dissolve the caramelized sugar.
If using yerba mate or strong black tea, steep it separately and stir a splash into the hot milk; otherwise the caramelized milk alone is a fine, simpler version.
Serve hot, first thing in the morning.

name: Paraguayan Sopa Paraguaya
steps: Saute the onion until soft and sweet, then let cool slightly.
Mix the corn flour, cheese, egg, and onion with enough milk or water to form a thick, pourable batter.
Pour into a small greased baking dish and bake at 375F for 30-35 minutes until set and golden - this is a dense savory cornbread, not a soup, despite the name.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion in the vegetable oil over medium heat for 6-8 minutes until very soft and sweet, then set aside to cool slightly.
In a bowl, whisk the eggs with the melted butter and milk.
Stir in the corn flour, crumbled queso fresco, and cooled onion, mixing until you have a thick, spoonable batter - add a splash more milk if it seems too stiff.
Pour into a small greased baking dish and smooth the top.
Bake at 375F (190C) for 30-35 minutes until the top is golden and a toothpick comes out clean.
Let it rest 10 minutes before cutting into squares - it firms up as it cools.

name: Paraguayan So'o Josopy
steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Add the diced tomatoes, rice, and 2 cups water, and simmer 20 minutes until the rice is tender and the soup has thickened.
Season and serve hot.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground beef with the onion, bell pepper, garlic, and cumin over medium-high heat, 6-8 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes and beef broth, and bring to a simmer.
Stir in the rice and simmer, partially covered, 20-22 minutes until the rice is tender and the soup has thickened to a hearty, porridge-like consistency.
Stir in the cilantro just before serving hot.

name: Paraguayan Bori Bori
steps: Simmer the chicken in water with the carrot for 20 minutes until cooked through; shred the meat and return it to the broth.
Mix the corn flour with the cheese and a little broth to form a stiff paste, then roll into small dumplings.
Drop the dumplings into the simmering broth and cook 8-10 minutes until they float and are cooked through.
steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken thigh with the onion, carrot, and chicken broth for 20-25 minutes until the chicken is cooked through.
Remove the chicken, shred it, and return it to the pot.
Mix the corn flour with the crumbled queso fresco and a few tablespoons of the hot broth, kneading briefly until you have a stiff, cohesive paste.
Roll the paste into small balls (about 1 inch), and drop them into the simmering broth.
Cook 8-10 minutes until the dumplings float to the surface and are cooked through in the center.
Stir in the cilantro and serve hot in bowls.

name: Paraguayan Guiso de Fideos

ingredients.ingredient: Pasta, cooked, enriched, with added salt

steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Add the diced tomatoes and 1 cup water, and simmer 10 minutes.
Stir in the cooked pasta and simmer 5 more minutes until everything is heated through and slightly thickened.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Pasta, cooked, enriched, with added salt

steps_detailed: Brown the ground beef with the onion, bell pepper, and garlic over medium-high heat, 6-7 minutes.
Add the diced tomatoes, beef broth, and oregano, and bring to a simmer for 8-10 minutes to let the sauce come together.
Stir in the cooked pasta and simmer 5-6 more minutes, stirring occasionally, until everything is well combined and heated through.
Top with the crumbled queso fresco before serving.

name: Paraguayan Asado

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Season the beef generously with salt.
Grill or pan-sear over high heat until well charred outside and cooked to your liking inside.
Rest 5 minutes, then slice and serve with a quick herb sauce made from the cilantro, oil, and garlic stirred together.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Season the beef generously with salt, black pepper, and half the minced garlic, and let it sit at room temperature 15-20 minutes.
Grill over high heat (or sear in a very hot heavy skillet), 4-6 minutes per side depending on thickness, until well charred outside and cooked to your preferred doneness.
Meanwhile, stir together the cilantro, oil, remaining garlic, and vinegar for a quick herb sauce.
Rest the meat 5-8 minutes before slicing against the grain.
Serve with the herb sauce spooned over the top.

name: Paraguayan Milanesa
steps: Season the pounded chicken and dredge in flour, then beaten egg.
Fry in the oil 3-4 minutes per side until golden and cooked through.
Serve with rice or a simple salad.
steps_detailed: Pound the chicken breast to an even 1/4-inch thickness and season both sides with salt, black pepper, and the minced garlic.
Set up a dredging station: flour in one dish, beaten eggs in another, and cornmeal (standing in for the traditional breadcrumbs) in a third.
Coat each piece of chicken in flour, then egg, then the cornmeal, pressing gently to help it adhere.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium-high heat and fry the chicken 3-4 minutes per side until deeply golden and cooked through.
Drain briefly on a paper towel before serving.

name: Paraguayan Puchero
steps: Simmer the chicken in water 20 minutes until nearly cooked through.
Add the cassava and simmer 15 minutes, then add the corn and cabbage and simmer 8-10 more minutes.
Serve hot in the broth.
steps_detailed: Combine the chicken, onion, bay leaf, and 2.5 cups water in a pot. Simmer 20 minutes until the chicken is nearly cooked through.
Add the cassava and sweet potato, and simmer 15 minutes.
Add the corn and cabbage, and continue simmering 8-10 minutes until all the vegetables are tender.
Remove the bay leaf, stir in the cilantro, and serve hot with the broth and vegetables together.

name: Paraguayan Locro Paraguayo
steps: Brown the pork with the onion.
Add the hominy, tomatoes, and 1.5 cups water, and simmer 25-30 minutes until the pork is tender and the stew has thickened.
Season and serve hot.
steps_detailed: Brown the pork shoulder with the onion, bell pepper, garlic, and cumin over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the hominy, diced tomatoes, and 2 cups water. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and simmer 25-30 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the pork is tender and the stew has thickened.
Stir in the cilantro just before serving.

name: Paraguayan Chipa Guasu
steps: Blend the corn kernels roughly - some texture is good, it shouldn't be totally smooth.
Mix with the crumbled cheese, egg, and milk.
Pour into a small greased baking dish and bake at 375F for 30-35 minutes until set and golden on top.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion in a little of the butter until soft, then let cool slightly.
Pulse the corn kernels in a food processor or blender briefly - you want a coarse, textured puree, not a smooth one.
Combine the corn, crumbled queso fresco, eggs, milk, cooled onion, and remaining melted butter in a bowl and mix well.
Pour into a small greased baking dish and smooth the top.
Bake at 375F (190C) for 30-38 minutes until golden and set with a slight jiggle only in the very center.
Let rest 10 minutes before cutting into squares.

name: Paraguayan Empanadas
steps: Brown the beef with the onion and season well.
Fill the tortillas with the beef mixture and chopped egg, fold and seal the edges.
Bake at 400F for 15-18 minutes until golden, or pan-fry until crisp.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion and bell pepper in the oil until soft, then add the ground beef and cumin, browning fully, 6-8 minutes. Let cool.
Stir the chopped egg and olives into the cooled beef mixture.
Make a simple dough: rub the butter into the flour with a pinch of salt, then add warm water gradually until a smooth dough forms. Rest 15 minutes, then roll and cut into rounds.
Fill each round with the beef mixture, fold into a half-moon, and crimp the edges to seal well.
Bake at 400F (200C) for 18-22 minutes until golden brown, or fry in oil until crisp if you prefer.

name: Paraguayan Pastel Mandi'o
steps: Boil the cassava until very tender, then mash smooth.
Brown the beef with the onion and season well.
Shape the mashed cassava around a spoonful of the beef filling into small turnovers, then fry in the oil until golden on all sides.
steps_detailed: Boil the cassava in salted water until completely tender, 20-25 minutes, then drain and mash smooth while warm.
Saute the onion and garlic until soft, then add the ground beef and cumin, browning fully, 6-8 minutes. Stir in the chopped egg and let cool.
Take a portion of the mashed cassava, flatten it in your palm, and place a spoonful of the beef filling in the center.
Fold the cassava around the filling to fully enclose it, shaping into a small oval turnover, patching any cracks with a bit more mashed cassava.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the turnovers on all sides, 2-3 minutes per side, until golden and crisp.
Drain on a paper towel before serving.

name: Paraguayan Kivevé
steps: Boil the sweet potato (standing in for pumpkin/squash) until tender, then mash.
Mix in the corn flour, cheese, and brown sugar, then cook over low heat, stirring, until it thickens into a soft, spoonable mash.
Serve warm.
steps_detailed: Boil the sweet potato until very tender, 15-18 minutes, then drain and mash smooth.
Stir in the corn flour, crumbled queso fresco, brown sugar, milk, and butter.
Cook over low heat, stirring constantly, 8-10 minutes until it thickens into a soft, custardy, spoonable mash - the cheese should mostly melt in, giving a sweet-savory balance.
Serve warm as a side dish or light dessert.

name: Paraguayan Dulce de Mamón
steps: Simmer the papaya nectar with the brown sugar and cinnamon until reduced to a thick, syrupy consistency, about 15-20 minutes.
Traditionally made with green papaya chunks candied slowly in syrup - this simplified version captures the same sweet, mildly floral flavor.
Cool and serve as a small sweet spoonful, often with cheese alongside.
steps_detailed: Combine the papaya nectar, brown sugar, and cinnamon in a small saucepan.
Simmer over medium-low heat, stirring occasionally, for 20-25 minutes until reduced by about half into a thick, glossy syrup.
Let cool to room temperature - it will thicken further as it sits.
Serve in small spoonfuls with a bit of crumbled queso fresco alongside, the traditional sweet-and-savory pairing.

name: Paraguayan Arroz Kesu
steps: Simmer the rice in the milk with the sugar until very tender and creamy, about 20-25 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Stir in the crumbled cheese until it softens into the warm rice.
Serve warm or chilled.
steps_detailed: Combine the rice, milk, sugar, and cinnamon in a saucepan and bring to a gentle simmer.
Cook over low heat, stirring occasionally, for 22-28 minutes until the rice is very tender and the mixture has thickened to a creamy pudding consistency.
Remove from heat and stir in the crumbled queso fresco until it softens and partially melts into the warm pudding.
Serve warm, or chill for a firmer, cooler dessert.

name: Paraguayan Salsa Criolla
steps: Combine the diced tomato, onion, and bell pepper in a bowl.
Stir in the oil and season with salt and a splash of vinegar if you have it.
Let sit 10 minutes before serving over grilled meat.
steps_detailed: Finely chop the tomato, onion, and bell pepper and combine in a bowl.
Stir in the oil, vinegar, and cilantro, and season well with salt.
Let it sit at room temperature for 15-20 minutes so the vegetables soften slightly and the flavors meld.
Spoon over grilled or fried meats.

name: Paraguayan Chimichurri
steps: Combine the cilantro, garlic, oil, and vinegar in a bowl.
Season generously with salt and a pinch of dried oregano if you have it.
Let sit at least 15 minutes before serving for the flavors to develop.
steps_detailed: Finely chop the cilantro and combine with the minced garlic and jalapeno in a bowl.
Whisk in the oil, vinegar, and oregano, and season generously with salt.
Let sit at room temperature at least 20-30 minutes before serving so the flavors fully develop.
Spoon generously over grilled meats.

name: Paraguayan Salsa de Maní

ingredients.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Whisk the peanut butter with the warm broth until smooth and pourable.
Stir in the garlic and lime juice, and season with salt.
Serve warm over grilled meat or vegetables.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanut Butter, smooth (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Warm the chicken broth gently in a small saucepan.
Whisk in the peanut butter a little at a time until fully smooth and pourable, adding a touch more broth if it's too thick.
Stir in the garlic, jalapeno, and lime juice, and season with salt.
Simmer 2-3 minutes to let the garlic mellow slightly, then serve warm over grilled meats or steamed vegetables.
